segments.s615-g137: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. هر کشوری is a genitive-like phrase meaning 'every/each country' (هر + noun). باژ نو is a noun + adjective order ('a new cry'), and خواستند is the 3rd-person plural past tense of خواستن; here it functions as 'they raised/sent forth' (literary use: they desired/caused a cry to be made). The subject 'they' is plural and is carried through to the next clause.
segments.s615-g138: زمین را shows the definite object marked by the object particle را ('the earth'). به + noun (به دیبا) indicates the manner/instrument ('in/with brocade'), and بیاراستند is the 3rd-person plural past of بیاراستن ('they adorned/embellished'). Word order is object before verb (typical SOV); the plural subject is implied from the previous clause.
segments.s615-g139: برین‌گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this way/thus'. یک چند is an idiomatic quantifier 'for a short time / for a while' (here adverbial). گیتی is the subject 'the world', and بخورد is 3rd-person singular (here literary/archaic) meaning 'befalls/comes upon' or 'experiences' (bchورد used impersonally: 'it happened to the world'). The clause therefore means 'thus for a while befell the world...'.
segments.s615-g140: A series of prepositional phrases introduced by repeated به ('in/for') lists the kinds of experience: به بزم (in feasting), به رزم (in battle), به ننگ (in shame), و نبرد (and battle/struggle). The repeated به emphasizes each domain; these nouns function as complements to بخورد ('the world experienced/in these respects').
segments.s615-g49: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن زخم is the noun phrase 'that wound' (آن = 'that', زخم = 'wound') and دیدند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they saw'; بر here means 'on' and introduces the locus (بر ماده گور = 'on the female onager'), with ماده 'female (animal)' modifying گور 'onager'. Word order is typical Persian: temporal conjunction + object + verb, then a prepositional phrase indicating where the wound was seen.
segments.s615-g50: خردمند is the subject 'the wise (man)'. گفت is the verb 'said' (3rd-person past) introducing reported speech. اینت is an archaic/dialectal demonstrative functioning like 'this is' as the predicate introducer; شمشیر و زور are the nominal predicate 'sword and might' (two nouns linked by و). The clause thus reports the wise man's judgment: 'This is sword and force.'
segments.s615-g51: مبیناد is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person-present/jussive-form meaning 'may it appear / may it be seen' (here part of a wish-like clause). Its object is چشم بد 'the evil eye' and این شاه را is the topical/affected phrase 'on/for this king' marked by را. In context the clause expresses a wish/concern concerning the king and the 'evil eye'.
segments.s615-g52: نماند is the verb 'remain / be left' (3rd-person) used with the negative sense in context ('nothing will remain'); به جز = 'except'; بر فلک 'on the firmament/sky' gives the location; ماه را ('the moon' with the object/topic marker را) is the item that is excepted. Together: 'nothing will remain except the moon upon the sky.' The particle order indicates the exception (به جز ... را) applies to what is left.
segments.s615-g53: سر مهتران جهان is a genitive construction 'the heads of the great men of the world' (سر = 'head' used collectively; مهتران = 'great/elderly ones' here 'chiefs'); زیر اوست places them with the predicate 'are beneath him' (زیر = 'under', اوست = archaic copula 'is/are'). Word order places the possessed phrase before the predicate, typical in Persian poetic style.
segments.s615-g54: فلک is the subject 'the sky/firmament'. زیر + (پیکان و شمشیر) اوست is the predicate 'is under his arrow and sword' where پیکان 'arrow' and شمشیر 'sword' are conjoined by و and owned by او 'his'. ست is the poetic copula (is). The clause emphasizes cosmic subjection to his weapons.
segments.s615-g55: سپاه is the subject 'the army'. از پس‌اندر is a composite adverbial meaning 'afterwards / from behind', and همی is an archaic continuous/aspective particle (here marking immediate action), while تاختند is the 3rd-person plural past 'they charged/rushed/attacked'. Together the phrase describes the army's immediate onward charge.
segments.s615-g56: بیابان is 'the desert/plain' and ز (poetic 'از') means 'from'. گوران here is a plural noun; given the immediate context it may refer to a group/places associated with 'گور', but grammatically it is the source phrase 'from the guran/graves/Guran'. بپرداختند is the verb (past plural) used here in a dynamic sense 'they sprang/leapt/attacked' in this poetic context. The clause describes a movement across the open country originating from گوران.
segments.s615-g25: هم‌انگاه is an adverb meaning 'immediately' modifying the whole clause. The phrase گور اندر آمد به سر is a typical older Persian order where the prepositional element (گور اندر) precedes the verb آمد; اندر + آمد = 'came in/onto'. به سر here marks the target ('to/onto the head'), so literally 'the grave came onto (his) head' — an idiom meaning the grave approached him (i.e., death neared).
segments.s615-g26: برفتند is past, 3rd-person plural ('they went/advanced'). In this poetic line the verb precedes the following noun phrase گردان زرین کمر, which names the subject/group affected: 'a troop (گردان) with golden belts (زرین کمر)'. Thus the segment means 'the golden‑belted troop moved forward.'
segments.s615-g27: شگفت here functions as a noun/nominal exclamation 'amazement' or 'they were amazed'. اندرآن (contracted here as اندر + ان) points to 'at that' (i.e., at that wound), so اندر آن زخم او = 'at that wound of his.' ماندند is past, 3rd-person plural 'they remained/stood (there),' so the clause says 'they stood amazed at his wound.'
segments.s615-g28: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one.' برو آفرین is a fixed exclamatory formula equivalent to 'bravo!' in which برو functions as an interjection rather than its literal meaning 'go.' خواندند is past 3rd-person plural 'they called/proclaimed,' so the phrase means 'one by one they cried “bravo.”'
segments.s615-g29: که introduces the clause ('that' / 'so that'); کس here is used in the negative sense with ندید, i.e., 'no one.' The coordinated noun phrase پر و پیکان تیرش lists parts ('feather and shaft of his arrow'), and ندید is past negative ('did not see / saw not'), so the clause reads 'that no one saw the feather or shaft of his arrow.'
segments.s615-g30: به بالای آن گور is a prepositional phrase 'to/above that grave' (به + بالای + آن). شد ناپدید is a two-word predicate where شد ('became') combines with the adjective ناپدید ('vanished/invisible') — literally 'became vanished' = 'vanished.' The whole clause: 'he vanished above that grave.'
segments.s615-g31: This is a coordinated noun phrase: سواران جنگی = 'mounted/military riders' (noun + postpositive adjective), و مردان کین = 'and men of vengeance' (men + postpositive qualifier). The adjectives follow the nouns in this classical Persian construction.
segments.s615-g32: سراسر is an adverb 'throughout' or 'all around.' As above, برو functions as part of the exclamatory formula 'برو آفرین' rather than literal 'go.' خواندند آفرین: 'they called/cried “bravo.”' The sequence therefore means 'throughout they shouted “bravo.”'
segments.s615-g105: گشادند is a 3rd-person plural past verb meaning ‘they opened’ or ‘were opened’; زان پس is a fused temporal phrase ‘after that / thereafter’ (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'); در گنج باز is a locative phrase (‘in the treasure-house’), where باز functions as an adjective ‘open’. The clause therefore reads literally ‘they were opened thereafter in the treasury.’
segments.s615-g106: توانگر شد is a simple past construction ‘became wealthy’ (توانگر here adjectival/nominative). آنکس که introduces a relative clause ‘the one who’; بودش نیاز is an older idiomatic order meaning ‘need was to him’ = ‘he was in need’ (بودش uses the enclitic -ش ‘to him/his’). Together: ‘the one who had been in need became rich.’
segments.s615-g107: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’; نخچیرگه is the place-name ‘hunting-ground’; سوی + place is the direction-marker ‘toward (Baghdad)’; رفت is simple past ‘went’. Word order here is poetic but literal: ‘from the hunting-ground toward Baghdad went.’
segments.s615-g108: خرد یافته is a participial phrase ‘having acquired wisdom’ (خرد = wisdom, یافته = acquired/found). با دلی شاد is a prepositional phrase ‘with a joyful heart’ (دلی = a heart/heartfelt; شاد modifies it). The final رفت repeats the verb ‘went’ for cadence: ‘having gained wisdom, with a joyful heart he went.’
segments.s615-g109: برفتند is 3rd-person plural past ‘they went/advanced’; گردنکشان is the plural noun ‘those who raise their necks’ (i.e. rebels/proud ones); پیش اوی = ‘before him/in his presence’ where اوی is the pronominal clitic. The verb-first order (برفتند … پیش اوی) is typical of the epic/poetic style.
segments.s615-g110: ز = ‘from’; بیگانه = ‘strangers/foreigners’; و آنک introduces a following descriptive clause ‘and those who…’ (آنک = ‘those who/that which’); بد خویش اوی = ‘of bad disposition belonging to him’ — خوی takes the enclitic -ش ‘his’ and اوی again refers to him/them. The whole phrase means ‘from strangers and from those of his own who were ill-disposed.’
segments.s615-g111: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past ‘he commanded/said’; تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ‘that/so that’; بازگردد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive ‘may/should return’ (classical use after تا); سپاه is the subject ‘the army’. Word order is inverted for poetic effect: ‘he commanded that the army return.’
segments.s615-g112: بیامد is past ‘(it) came’ (here the army is treated as a collective subject); به introduces the destination ‘to/into’; کاخ دلارای شاه is a genitival phrase ‘the palace of the beloved king’ (دلارای is an epithet ‘beloved’ modifying the king or the palace as epithet). The clause reads ‘the host came to the beloved king’s palace.’
segments.s615-g89: This clause names the subject (گنه‌کار, “the sinner”) followed by the present-tense verb دارد (dārad, “has”). بدان is the fused prepositional phrase به + آن meaning “about/with respect to that,” and چیز رای = “that thing’s opinion/plan” (چیز “thing” modifies رای “opinion”). Word order is Subject – Verb – prepositional phrase + noun: “The sinner has, about that thing, an opinion.”
segments.s615-g90: ندارد is the present third‑person negative of داشتن (nadarad = “does not have”). It governs the following locative/inner phrase به دل (“in [his] heart”), followed by the coordinated nouns شرم و بیم خدای (“shame and the fear of God”), i.e. things he lacks inwardly. The negative verb applies to the whole nominal phrase: “He does not have in his heart shame and God‑fearing.”
segments.s615-g91: سخن (sukhan) “word/utterance” is the thing being referred to; زین here functions as زِ این (“of/from this” or “about this”) modifying نشان (“sign, token”). کس is “anyone/any person.” مدارید is an older/poetic verb form; here it functions as an admonition not to do something (imperative/optative sense in context). باز “again” follows and reinforces “do not make it public again.” The line therefore commands secrecy: “Do not make this sign/word known to anyone again.”
segments.s615-g92: که introduces the reason/explanation clause (“for/because”). از + رازداران = “from/amid the secret‑keepers/confidants”; منم is emphatic “I am” (من + enclitic ـم), and بی‌نیاز is a predicate adjective meaning “free of need / self‑sufficient.” Grammatically this is a verbless explanatory clause: “for I am among the keepers of secrets and need none.”
segments.s615-g93: This clause uses a verb of causing/making: کنم (konam, “I make” / 1st person present/subjunctive) with توانگر as the predicate applied to the object مرد درویش را (the dervish/poor man, marked by the object marker را). Word order is Verb (کنم) after the predicate adjective (توانگر), typical of classical Persian poetic inversion: “I make the dervish a rich man.”
segments.s615-g94: به دین آورم is a causative construction: به دین = “to the religion/faith” and آورم (āvaram, “I bring/bring about”) is first person singular. جان بدکیش را (the life/soul of the ill‑fated one, marked by را) is the object being brought to the faith. Together: “I bring the soul/life of the ill‑fated to the faith,” i.e. I convert or save the ill‑fated person’s soul.
segments.s615-g95: بتوزیم is an archaic/poetic verb form (here functioning as a first‑person plural or hortative/optative) related to ‘give/distribute’; the exact nuance is obscure in modern Persian. فام can mean “hue/trace,” and کسی is “anyone”; کش here behaves like an agentive/derivative element or part of a compound. درم is the small coin (dirham), the object tied to distribution. Grammatically the clause refers to giving/distributing a dirham (or not) to someone; the precise idiom is archaic, so senses are uncertain.
segments.s615-g96: نباشد here expresses non‑existence/absence (literally “may there not be”/“there is not”), and it links back to درم in the previous segment: “there is no dirham/money.” دل خویش دارد به غم is a simple present clause: دل خویش (“his own heart” where خوی + ـش = his) دارد به غم = “has sorrow” / “is full of grief.” Put together: “[there is] no dirham; his heart has sorrow.”
segments.s615-g65: ازان is the poetic fused form of از آن ('from those/that'); placed before شهرها it yields 'from those towns'. هرک is an indefinite relative 'whoever/anyone who' and the copula بود (past) comes at the end, so the clause reads literally 'from those towns whoever a dervish was'. This is a common classical word order where the predicate (بود) closes the clause.
segments.s615-g66: وگر is a conditional/contrastive particle ('or/but if') introducing an alternative description. نانش = نان + -ش is 'his bread' (his livelihood). از کوشش خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from his own exertion' where خویش is the reflexive/possessive idea built from خوی + -ش; the clause ends with بود (past 'was'), so literally 'and if his bread was from his own effort'.
segments.s615-g67: ز is the poetic variant of از meaning 'by/through'; بخشیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'giving' and او marks its possessor ('his giving'). توانگر شدند is a past-result clause 'they became wealthy' with شدند (third-person plural past of شدن) as the predicator; the subject ('they') is implied from the previous context.
segments.s615-g68: بسی نیز functions as an adverbial/quantifier 'many/much also' or 'moreover', followed by a with‑phrase با تخت و افسر 'with throne and helmet' (i.e. possessing thrones and helmets). شدند again is the past copula/verb 'they became' — together: 'many likewise became (men) with thrones and helmets.' Note افسر here means 'helmet' (royal headgear) rather than 'officer'.
segments.s615-g69: به ... اندر آمد is an archaic/perhaps emphatic word order meaning 'came into the city' (به = to/into, اندر = in/within). ز نخچیرگاه 'from the hunting-ground' marks the origin. آمد is the simple past 'came'.
segments.s615-g70: بکی هفته is a temporal phrase 'for a short while/about a week' (بکی = a little, هفته = week). بد here is a poetic/adverbial particle occurring in classical usage as part of the temporal expression (it is not intended as a literal negative here) and shadman 'شادمان' is the predicate adjective 'rejoicing/happy'; با سپاه 'with the army' indicates accompaniment. The clause thus reads 'for about a week he was rejoicing with the army.'
segments.s615-g71: برفتی is a past verb form meaning 'went forth/went away' in this poetic diction; the final -ی can appear in older registers. خوش‌آواز is a compound adjective 'pleasant-sounding / melodious' placed before the noun, and گوینده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a speaker/storyteller' (گوینده + -ای). Together they form 'a melodious-voiced speaker went forth.'
segments.s615-g72: خردمند 'wise' and درویش 'dervish' are epithets in apposition to the speaker; جوینده‌ای (جوینده + -ای) is 'a seeker' or 'inquirer' with an indefinite ending. The sequence lists qualities/appellations of that person: 'a wise man and a dervish seeker.'
segments.s615-g41: برانگیخت is a past‑tense 3rd‑person singular verb 'he/she/it raised/roused' placed at the clause start (poetic inversion). آن بارکش را is the definite object (بارکش 'beast of burden') marked by the object marker را; ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from its place' attached to the verb.
segments.s615-g42: تو گفتی is the 2nd‑person past 'you said' (reporting verb). شد is the verb 'became' introducing the reported/result clause; in poetry the reported clause is split, so آن باره پران همای is the content of what is said: آن باره 'that load/that thing', پران 'winged' (adjective), همای the mythical bird. Word order is inverted for emphasis.
segments.s615-g43: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/someone' here starting the clause. گور is the animal 'onager' (the subject). پیش آمدش is a compound: پیش 'near/forward' + آمدش 'came to/approached it' (past). ماده بود is a predicative 'it was a female (animal)'; بود is simple past copula.
segments.s615-g44: بچه 'the foal/young' is the topic; پیش ازو رفته is a past perfective sequence meaning 'had gone ahead of her' (پیش 'ahead', ازو 'from/of her', رفته 'gone'). او مانده بود is 'she had remained/was left behind' (مانده participle + بود auxiliary gives past perfect sense).
segments.s615-g45: یکی here 'someone' introduces an unspecified agent; تیغ زد is the verbal phrase 'struck (with a) sword' (تیغ noun + زد 'struck'). بر میانش is a locative 'at/on its middle' (بر 'on/upon' + میانش 'its middle'). سوار at the end identifies the agent as 'a rider' (poetic placement of the agent after the verb).
segments.s615-g46: بدونیم is a poetic/compound form built on بدون 'without' with an enclitic implication (here functioning as a predicative idea 'bereft/without [something]' or 'made without'), followed by شد 'became' (past). گور is the subject 'the onager' and ناپایدار is the adjective 'unstable, unsteady' describing its resulting state.
segments.s615-g47: رسیدند is the 3rd‑person plural past 'they arrived/reached'. نزدیک او is the adverbial/prepositional 'near him'. مهتران is the plural noun 'chiefs/great men' (poetic/archaic plural); subject and locative are arranged in the compact, elevated word order typical of epic verse.
segments.s615-g48: سرافراز 'proud/lofty' and شمشیر زن is a compound meaning 'sword‑wielding' or 'swordsmen' (شمشیر 'sword' + زن 'one who strikes' used as an agentive). کهتران qualifies them as the lesser/younger group (poetic comparative/relational plural); the adjectives follow the head noun in the condensed style of the poem.
segments.s615-g81: وگر is the conditional conjunction 'if'. The phrase follows a somewhat inverted order: وام دارد کسی زین گروه = 'a loan/debt (وام) has (دارد) someone (کسی) of this company (زین گروه)'; زین here is an archaised/contracted form (ز + این) meaning 'of this/from this'.
segments.s615-g82: شده‌ست is perfective/passive-ish 'has become' describing the subject (کسی). از here marks cause/agent ('from/by' or 'because of'), followed by بد وام‌خواهان 'bad creditors/claimants' as the cause; ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary'. The clause therefore says he 'has become weary because of bad creditors.'
segments.s615-g83: وگر starts the conditional. بی‌پدر modifies کودکانند: بی‌پدر 'fatherless' + کودکانند (کودکان + -ند) 'they are children'; نیز 'also/too' links this case to the previous one.
segments.s615-g84: ازان 'from/of that' introduces the beneficiary/target: کس که دارد 'the person who has (it)'; بخواهند is 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/future 'they will ask/will desire' and چیز is the object 'things' — together: 'they will ask things from that man who has [it].'
segments.s615-g85: بود here is existential/'be' ('if there is' / 'were there'), followed by مام 'mother' and کودک نهفته 'a hidden child' with نیاز 'in need' — the whole segment sets up the condition 'if there is a mother and a hidden needy child.'
segments.s615-g86: بدوبر is an adverbial numeral 'twofold/twice'. گشایم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I open / I will open' (poetic form). در گنج باز = 'in/into the treasure (vault) open(ly)' — here باز functions as 'open' (i.e., I will open the treasure twice).
segments.s615-g87: وگر introduces another conditional. مایه‌داری and توانگر are synonymous adjectives/nouns describing a rich person; بمرد is the verb 'to die' in a subjunctive/optative form here functioning as 'dies' in the protasis ('if ... dies').
segments.s615-g88: بدین (here split as ب + دین) + مرز = 'in/at this border/realm' (بدین is an archaising/demonstrative compound). ازو 'from him' marks possession‑source; کودکان ماند 'children remain / are left' with ماند as 'be left'; خرد modifies کودکان as 'small/young'. The clause means his young children will be left in that land/realm.
segments.s615-g9: This is a short reported-speech lead-in: 'یکی پهلوان گفت' = 'a certain champion said.' The verb گفت is the simple past 'said' placed after its subject (typical Persian order). Immediately after this comes the vocative 'کای شهریار' ('O king'), where کای is the archaic/epic vocative particle and شهریار is the addressee 'king.'
segments.s615-g10: نگه کن is an imperative phrase 'look!' or 'keep your gaze (on)'; نگه is a nominal root 'gaze' and کن is the imperative auxiliary 'do'. بدین لشکر is a prepositional/demonstrative phrase meaning 'at/on this host' (بدین = به + این, 'to/at this'), followed by لشکر 'army/host' and the adjective نامدار 'renowned'. The order is imperative + object phrase, typical for a direct command.
segments.s615-g11: This clause begins with the relative/introducing particle که and contains the question-like phrase 'با کیست' = 'with whom is it?' (با + کی + ست, where ست is the copula 'is' attached to کی 'who'). The noun phrase 'زین‌گونه تیر و کمان' ('such arrows and bows') follows; word order places the locative/oblique ('with whom') close to the copula while the qualifying phrase 'زین‌گونه تیر و کمان' specifies the thing under discussion.
segments.s615-g12: This is an elliptical, compact comment: 'بداندیش‌گر مرد' = 'an ill‑minded (evil‑intentioned) man' (adjective + noun). 'نیکی گمان' is terse: literally 'good (نیکی) thought/expectation (گمان)' — read idiomatically as an admonition not to suppose good of him (i.e. 'expect no good'). Persian epic often omits verbs in such tags, so the two parts form an appositive judgment about the person just asked about.
segments.s615-g13: مگر here functions rhetorically (close to 'perhaps' or a wishful formula) and is followed by باشد in an optative/subjunctive sense ('may it be'). 'این را' ('this' marked by the object/topic particle را) is the thing wished for, and 'گشاد برت' ('wide/ample upon you') is a short predicative phrase meaning 'may it be broad/ample for you' (برت = بر + ت, 'upon you').
segments.s615-g14: که introduces the result/purpose clause ('that/so that'). بادا is the optative auxiliary 'may it be', so the phrase 'جاوید بادا سر و افسرت' reads as a blessing: 'may your head and your افسرت (helmet) be eternal.' جاوید is 'eternal' and سر و افسرت ('head and helmet') are the two things wished to endure.
segments.s615-g15: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/as/if'. تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you', and the verb phrase is built with the noun + گیر(ی): 'تیر گیری' = 'you take/seize arrows' (گیری = present/you form of گرفتن). The coordination 'و شمشیر و گرز' lists additional weapons 'and sword and mace' that you take up; the clause sets up a condition/time for what follows.
segments.s615-g16: ازان = 'from that' (compound of از + آن) introduces the source/consequence; خسروی فر is a genitive-like collocation 'royal splendour/glory' (خسروی 'royal' qualifying فر 'glory, divine royal radiance'), and بالای برز = 'the high/exalted summit/eminence' (بالای = 'above, superior', برز = 'high/exalted'). Together they describe the lofty royal status or glory associated with the preceding weapons/act.
segments.s615-g97: دگر (an emphatic/contrastive particle) + هرک (classical contraction of هر که, 'whoever') precede the verb دارد. دارد is present/habitual 'has' (here 'harbors/possesses'). نهفته is a participial/adjectival form 'hidden' modifying the noun نیاز 'need/longing'. Word order is typical classical Persian: modifier(s) + noun + verb.
segments.s615-g98: همی is an archaic/prosodic marker meaning 'still/continually' and here marks habitual action; دارد is again 'keeps/has' (i.e. 'keeps secret'). از indicates source/relationship ('of'/'about'); تنگی here means 'distress/straiteness' (emotional or material suffering); خوی + enclitic ش together yield 'his own' (possessive); راز is the direct object 'secret'. The clause means 'he still keeps secret the reason of his own distress.'
segments.s615-g99: مر is a dative/benefactive pronoun 'to/for me' (classical placement before the object); او را is the definite object 'him' (را marks the object); ازان = 'from that' (of/from that affair); کار = 'affair/trouble'; بی‌غم 'without sorrow/carefree' modifies the result; کنم is first-person present/subjunctive used optatively/future ('I will make/ I will cause'). Overall: 'I will make him carefree of that affair.'
segments.s615-g100: فزون here functions as a verb-modifying element meaning 'more/increase' (Ferdowsi uses it with a following noun to mean 'to increase X'); شادی 'joy' is the thing to be increased. و links the two coordinated results. اندهش is the poet's form for 'his sorrow/grief' (parallel to شادی) and کم 'less' with the verb کنم yields 'I lessen (it)'. The clause means 'I will increase (his) joy and I will lessen (his) sorrow.'
segments.s615-g101: گر is the conditional 'if'; از marks source/agent 'from' or 'on the part of'; کارداران (kārdārān) are the officials/managers/stewards (plural); بود is past 'was/there was'; رنج 'hardship/suffering' is the noun; نیز 'also/even' adds emphasis. Word order is verb-final (classical): 'If from the officials was hardship also.'
segments.s615-g102: که introduces the explanatory/casual subordinate clause 'that/because'; او is the subject 'he'; از 'from' marks source of the favor/request; پدرمرده‌ای is an indefinite 'a fatherless man' (پدرمرده = one bereft of a father); خواست is past tense 'asked/wanted/desired'; چیز 'a thing/something' is the object placed after the verb for emphasis. Together: 'that he asked something of a fatherless man.'
segments.s615-g103: کنم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I will/do' used as an optative/future. زنده 'alive' modifies the action 'to hang alive'; بر دار (دار = gallows) expresses the action 'on the gallows' (بر دار کردن = to hang); بیداد here is personified as 'the unjust/tyrant/oppressor' and را marks it as the object. The clause means 'I will hang the oppressor alive.'
segments.s615-g104: که here gives the reason 'for/because' (sense 'because'); آزرد is past 'hurt/offended/ harmed'; او is the subject 'he'; مرد آزاد را is the object 'the free/noble man' with را marking it. Literally: 'because he harmed the free (noble) man.'
segments.s615-g129: ازان is an adverbial particle 'from that / thereupon', linking this sentence to the previous event. شاه ایران is the subject (noun + modifier). فراوان here functions adverbially ('greatly, very') modifying the following verb. ژکید is a poetic past-tense verb form meaning roughly 'was distressed/tormented' (an older/poetic verb form), so the clause reads literally 'thereupon the King of Iran was greatly distressed.'
segments.s615-g130: برآشفت is a past-tense verb 'he became angry / flew into a rage.' وز here functions as a brief connective/covering particle (in classical usage it can link the clauses; in the poetry it behaves like 'and/at') and introduces the next small phrase. روزبه names the person toward whom the emotion is directed, and لب گزید is an idiom 'to bite the lip' (گزید is past of گزیدن, here 'to bite'), describing a gesture of vexation. The whole segment: 'he became angry and, at Rouzbeh, bit his lip.'
segments.s615-g131: بدو is a prepositional/dative phrase 'to him' (shows the indirect object of گفت). گفت is the past of گفتن 'said' (here introducing reported speech). من is the first-person pronoun 'I' introducing the speaker's words. باژ is a noun 'cry / shout' (object), and روم و خزر names the targets/places ('to Rome and the Khazars') — the sequence is the reported clause 'I (من) باژ روم و خزر' = 'I will send/raise a cry to Rome and the Khazars.'
segments.s615-g132: بدیشان is 'to them / for them' (dative). چون here means 'as / like' (introducing the role or manner). بیاری is a noun from یار meaning 'a companion / ally' (here 'as a companion'). بدر is a proper name (Badr). Put together it reads 'I will give them Badr as a companion' — a concise, classical phrasing where the verb 'دهم' (to give) is carried from بدیشان دهم earlier in the clause.
segments.s615-g133: هم‌اکنون is an adverb 'right now / at once' setting the temporal frame. به + خروار دینار is a measure phrase 'a kharvar (large amount) of dinars' (به marks the quantity). خواه is the present/future desiderative/auxiliary 'ask / desire / demand' (classical usage standing for an imperative or hortative request): the whole segment means 'at once demand a kharvar of dinars.'
segments.s615-g134: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. گنج ری و اصفهان names the sources 'the treasury(ies) of Rey and Isfahan' (گنج = treasure/treasury; ری and اصفهان are place names). باژ خواه repeats the idea of announcing/ordering with a cry: باژ 'a cry/clamor' and خواه again 'ask/command' — together 'proclaim it with a cry / call it out.' The clause is an order to take money from the named treasuries and proclaim it aloud.
segments.s615-g135: شبستان 'bedchamber / inner chamber' is the subject. برین‌گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner / thus', and ویران is a predicative adjective 'ruined / desolate'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The structure is a simple past nominal sentence: 'The bedchamber was ruined thus.'
segments.s615-g136: نه negates the following idea ('not'). از here is the preposition 'by / from / because of' (poetic usage). اختر means 'star' (literal or figurative 'fortune'), followed by شاه ایران 'the King of Iran' as genitive modifier, and بود is 'was' (past). Together the clause says the ruin was not caused by the king of Iran's star/fortune: 'it was not by the King of Iran's star.'
segments.s615-g121: دو هفته (numeral + noun) means 'two weeks' and precedes the verb phrase. همی here is the classical/poetic continuative particle (≈ 'for / continuously'), and بود is the past copula 'was'. دل شادمان is a nominal predicate: دل 'heart' + post‑nominal adjective شادمان 'joyful' — literally 'the heart (was) joyful'.
segments.s615-g122: در گنج is a prepositional phrase 'in/within the treasure(vault)', placed before the verb (typical poetic word order). بگشاد is an archaic perfect/past form of 'to open' (literary), here meaning 'opened' (or 'was opened'). روز و شبان is a temporal adverbial 'day and night' (شبان is an archaic/plural form related to 'night' in poetry).
segments.s615-g123: درم 'dirham(s)/money' is the theme/object. داد و آمد is a compound of two past verbs 'gave and came' (a poetic idiom indicating money flowed/was distributed and arrived). به شهر صطخر is the destination prepositional phrase 'to the city Istakhr' placed after the verb cluster.
segments.s615-g124: به سر بر نهاد is a fixed classical sequence meaning 'placed upon the head': به سر 'to/on the head', بر 'on/upon' (preposition), نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place/put'. آن کیان is the subject 'that Kian (king)', and تاج فخر is a noun + qualifier 'the crown of pride' (تاج 'crown', فخر 'pride/glory').
segments.s615-g125: شبستاان (شبستان) is a palace/chamber or prayer‑hall; خود را is the reflexive/possessive 'his own' as an object/topic marker; چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction); در 'door' (here the physical door), باز 'open', and کرد is the past-tense auxiliary/verb 'did/made' — together در باز کرد = 'opened the door'. Word order (temporal clause first) is typical of classical narrative.
segments.s615-g126: بتان را is the definite object marker + noun: 'the betan' (here likely 'the beauties' in a poetic sense) as recipients. ز گنج = 'from the treasure' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). درم ساز کرد is a verbal sequence where ساز (literally 'device/means' or 'preparation') combines with کرد to mean 'made/prepared/gave coins' — i.e. he provided dirhams; کرد is the main past verb.
segments.s615-g127: به مشکوی زرین is an ablative/phrase meaning 'in/with musk-scented golden (cloth/thing)' (prepositional 'به' + adjectives مشکوی 'musk‑scented' and زرین 'golden'). هرانکس که is the relative/indefinite 'whoever / anyone who' and introduces a subordinate clause whose head noun (تاج) begins here and continues in the next segment.
segments.s615-g128: نبودش is the negative past 'was not' with the enclitic -ش referring back to the previously mentioned تاج (so 'whose crown was not' / 'if the crown was not [on him]'). بزیِر (بزیر) means 'down/under' (direction), اندرون 'the inner part / within', تخت عاج 'ivory throne' (تخت 'throne', عاج 'ivory'). The clause thus places something (understood from prior verbs) beneath the inner ivory throne for those without crowns.
segments.s615-g1: دگر روز 'the next/another day' is a temporal adverbial placed at the start. چون introduces a temporal clause ('when'); تاج is the definite object 'the crown' and بفروخت is a past tense verb with the old b- preform (here meaning 'gave up' or 'parted with'); the proper name هور follows the verb in the classical poetic word order (object + verb + subject).
segments.s615-g2: جهاندار شد is a simple past/resultative: جهاندار (a predicative noun 'sovereign') + شد 'became'. سوی introduces a directional phrase 'toward', followed by the noun phrase نخچیر گور where گور (onager) postmodifies نخچیر (prey/game) without an overt ezâfe in poetic diction; the whole prepositional phrase marks the direction of his movement.
segments.s615-g3: This is a participial/attributive clause: کمان را (the bow, with the object marker را) + به زه بر نهاده (literally 'having placed on the bowstring' — بر نهادن 'to place on/onto' in perfect participle نهاده) modifies سپاه (the army). In poetic order the participial phrase precedes the noun it qualifies, so the line means the army 'with bows set upon the strings'.
segments.s615-g4: پس is a temporal connector 'then/after that'. اندر = 'in/among' and together with لشکر (the host) forms the locative phrase. همی is the classical particle often used with verbs to signal ongoing or iterative action (here with رفت); رفت is simple past 'went'. شاه at the end is the subject in inverted poetic order: 'the king went into the host.'
segments.s615-g5: چنین گفت 'thus he said' introduces reported speech; گفت is the past reporting verb, and what follows is an indefinite/clausal subject phrase هرکو ('whoever'). کمان را is the definite object (marked by را) and به دست is an instrumental phrase 'with the hand' (به used here in the 'by/with' sense). Word order places the indefinite subject and object before the verb of the subordinate clause.
segments.s615-g6: This is the subordinate result/conditional clause: بمالد is a present/subjunctive form 'rubs' or 'if he rubs' (here general/indefinite action). گشاید is a present/resultative verb 'it opens' (classical present form). به اندازه شست is a measure phrase 'by the measure/extent of a thumb' (به as a measure preposition + اندازه 'measure' + شست 'thumb').
segments.s615-g7: نباید + masdar (زدن) is an impersonal modal construction meaning 'one must not [do X]' or 'it should not be done'; here زدن تیر 'to shoot an arrow' (تیر is the object). جز 'except' introduces the sole permitted target phrase بر سرون where بر is the preposition 'at/on' and سرون (plural) is a poetic plural meaning 'cypresses' or 'tall ones' (a conventional archery target image).
segments.s615-g8: که is a relative pronoun 'which/that' linking back to سرون. از سینه is a source phrase 'from the chest' (از = from). پیکانش is 'its/one's arrow' (پیکان + possessive -ش), آید is present 'comes' and برون is the adverbial 'out/ forth'. The relative clause specifies that the permitted targets are those at which the arrow will come out of the chest.
segments.s615-g17: همه لشکر is the noun phrase subject meaning 'the whole host' (همه modifying لشکر). از شاه is a prepositional phrase indicating the source or cause of the feeling ('of/before the king'). دارند شرم uses دارند (3rd person plural present of داشتن) with the noun شرم as its object—literally 'have shame', i.e. 'are ashamed.' Word order (subject — prepositional phrase — verb + noun) is typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s615-g18: ز تیر و کمانشان places the cause 'from/through their arrows and bows' (ز used poetically for 'from/because of'); کمانشان is کمان + possessive suffix شان 'their bow(s)'. شود is the present/future copular/verb 'become' (here 'grow' or 'turn'), and دست نرم is a predicate phrase literally 'hand soft' = 'the hand becomes weak/ineffectual'. The clause order is causal phrase → verb → predicate (poetic inversion).
segments.s615-g19: چنین داد پاسخ is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic sequence: چنین 'thus' + داد (past of دادن) 'gave' + پاسخ 'an answer' = 'gave such an answer / replied thus.' که introduces the reported content ('that/which'). این ایزدیست is a compact predicate: این 'this' + ایزدیست (adjective/copula cluster) 'is divine/of God' — in older Persian the copula can be fused with the adjective.
segments.s615-g20: کزو is a relative/prepositional compound 'from/than which' linking back to 'this' (این). بگذری is the classical/poetic present/subjunctive form of گذشتن used as 'to exceed/pass' — here functioning in a comparative sense ('by which you surpass/compared with which'). زور is the noun 'strength/might', بهرام is the proper name, and چی + ست (چیست) forms the interrogative 'what is' (چی 'what' + ست copula 'is'). The line thus asks rhetorically how Bahram's strength can compare.
segments.s615-g21: برانگیخت is the past tense verb 'roused/raised' (transitive). In poetry the verb can precede subject and object; here شبدیز is the (implied) subject and بهرام را (object + را) marks Bahram as the definite direct object. The construction means 'Shabdiz (the steed) raised Bahram' or 'Shabdiz reared, lifting Bahram.'
segments.s615-g22: همی is a frequent poetic adverb (here functioning as a temporal/emphatic 'then/thereupon'). تیز is an adjective/adverb 'swift/keen'; کرد is the past of کردن 'to make/do', so تیز کرد = 'made (it) swift' or 'spurred on.' او is the subject pronoun 'he' and دلارام را is the definite object (name + را). Overall: 'He then made Dolaram swift/urged Dolaram on.'
segments.s615-g23: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. آمدش is 'came to him' (past, with enclitic pronoun), and هنگام is the noun 'time/moment' — چو آمدش هنگام = 'when the time/moment came to him.' بگشاد is an archaic past form 'opened' (prefixed b- for past in classical Persian), and شست here means 'thumb/hand' (poetic use for the palm or hand), so بگشاد شست = 'he opened/spread his hand.'
segments.s615-g24: بر is the preposition 'on/upon', گور is 'grave' (object of action) and را is the definite object marker. با introduces the instrument 'with', and سرونش is سرون + possessive suffix (his/their cypresses/tall poles). ببست is the past tense 'he bound/tied/closed' (here 'bound up' or 'fastened' the grave with cypresses). The clause describes covering or securing the grave using cypresses.
segments.s615-g33: This is a short reported-speech frame: بدو is a dative/adverbial 'to him' (literally 'unto him'), پهلوان is the speaker 'the champion/hero', گفت is the simple past 'said', and کای شهریار is a vocative address 'O king' (کای = O, شهریار = king). Word order is slightly topicalized/poetic (dative then speaker then verb then vocative).
segments.s615-g34: مبیناد is an archaic/poetic verb form expressing the wish/jussive meaning 'may not see' (here negative wish: 'may ... not behold'); چشمت 'your eye' is the grammatical subject/experiencer; بد روزگار is a noun phrase 'bad days / ill fate' that serves as the thing not to be seen. The verb precedes the subject for poetic effect, and the whole clause is a benediction/curse formula ("may your eye not see bad times").
segments.s615-g35: سواری functions as a plural noun 'riders/horsemen'; تو و ما همه lists participants 'you and we all'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and خریم (خر + ـیم) means 'our donkeys' or 'donkeys' (we are mounted on asses). The construction states a contrast between the addressee's mounted force and the speaker's inferior mounts; the order 'سواری تو و ما همه' is emphatic/poetic.
segments.s615-g36: This is a comparative clause: هم 'even/also' emphasizes the comparison; از introduces the standard of comparison ('than' or 'compared with'); خروران appears to be a group/name (treated here as the standard being compared to); در هنر 'in skill' specifies the domain of comparison (در = 'in', هنر = 'skill'); کمتریم is the first-person-plural comparative predicate 'we are less' (کَمتَر + ـیم). Together: 'we are even less in skill than the Khururān.'
segments.s615-g37: بدو گفت شاه is an inverted, poetic word order meaning “the king said to him” (literally: ‘to him said the king’). این نه تیر منست is a nominative clause where این (this) precedes the negator نه (not) before the noun تیر (arrow), and منست is an archaic fused predicate (man + ast) functioning like “is mine”; together: “this is not my arrow.”
segments.s615-g38: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause (here: ‘for/that’ or ‘who’). پیروزگر is used as an epithet/noun meaning ‘the victor’ (the triumphant one), دستگیر here functions as a substantive meaning ‘helper/one who lends a hand’ (i.e. protector or succorer), and منست again is the archaic predicate ‘is mine’ (so: ‘the victor is my helper’).
segments.s615-g39: کرا is an archaic relative/interrogative form meaning ‘who’ (here: ‘there is no one who…’). پشت in this context means ‘support/protector’ (not bodily back), و is the coordinating ‘and’, یاور means ‘helper/ally’, جهاندار is an epithet for the sovereign/king (‘world-holder’), and نیست negates the whole predicate: ‘there is no one who is the sovereign’s backing and helper.’
segments.s615-g40: ازو is the enclitic/old form meaning ‘of him/from him’ (here ‘than him’ in a comparative sense), خوارتر is the comparative ‘more despicable’, در جهان means ‘in the world’, خوار is ‘despised/worthless’, and نیست negates: ‘there is no one in the world more despicable than he.’
segments.s615-g113: شبستان زرین بیاراستند is a past-tense clause: شبستان (noun) + زرین (adjective) = “golden bedchamber,” with the adjective following the noun as is common in classical Persian; بیاراستند is 3rd person plural past (“they adorned”), so the subject is understood from the verb.
segments.s615-g114: پرستندگان is the explicit plural subject “the worshippers.” رود here functions as a musical object/word in the celebration context (see following lines) and is linked by و to the verb phrase; می‌خواستند is 3rd person plural imperfect/past habitual (“they wished/desired” or “they meant to”), so the clause reads subject + (musical term) + conjunction + verbal desire.
segments.s615-g115: بتان is a poetic plural noun here meaning ‘the beauties’ or beloveds (metaphorical idols). چامه و چنگ is a coordinated object phrase (“verse/ode and harp”), and برساختند is 3rd person plural past: in poetic register it can mean both ‘built’ and ‘composed’ — here it denotes making/producing poems and musical instruments/strains (“they composed/made”).
segments.s615-g116: ز is the poetic form of از (“from”/“because of”); بیگانه is ‘stranger/foreigner’; ایوان is ‘iwan/portico’; بپرداختند is a past 3rd-person plural verb meaning they ‘attended to’ or ‘dealt with’ (or paid), so the clause says they dealt with / attended the stranger’s iwan. The clause continues the narrative action begun in the previous segment.
segments.s615-g117: ز introduces a source (“from”); رود in this musical context is best read as the stringed/melodic element, and می‌و (literary spelling) names fruit/wine as another source. بانگ چنگ و سرود is a coordinated noun phrase “the sound/cry of the harp and the song,” so the whole segment enumerates sources (strings and fruit) and resultant musical sounds.
segments.s615-g118: هوا را uses the object marker را to mark ‘the air/atmosphere’ as definite object; همی + داد is a repeated/continuous pastive idiom meaning ‘kept giving’ or ‘continually gave’; گفتی here stands for the act of utterance (in older usage akin to ‘they said’ or ‘the saying’), and درود is the noun ‘greeting’. Grammatically this yields ‘they continually gave the air a greeting’ (i.e. they kept greeting aloud).
segments.s615-g119: This is a series of prepositional/quantifying phrases: به هر شب = ‘each night’ (به marking temporal adverbial), ز هر حجره = ‘from every chamber’ (ز = poetic from), followed by the direct object یک دست‌بند = ‘one bracelet.’ Word order places the temporal and source phrases before the object.
segments.s615-g120: ببردند is 3rd person plural past (‘they took’ or ‘carried off’). تا here expresses purpose/result (‘so that’ / ‘until’), and the subordinate clause دل ندارد نژند reads دل (subject) + ندارد (present/result-state ‘does not have’) + نژند (predicative adjective ‘base/worthless’). Together it means they took these things nightly until there remained no base heart — i.e. until no heart retained baseness.
segments.s615-g57: This is a short clause with an indefinite subject + prepositional phrase and a past action verb. یکی مرد = an indefinite 'one/some man' placed before the noun (یکی functions like 'a certain/someone'). بر گرد لشکر is a locative phrase: بر + گرد (here گرد = 'around/about') gives 'around the army'. بگشت is a third-person past singular verb 'turned/went/returned around' (poetic/classical past form).
segments.s615-g58: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that' / 'that'). یک تن = 'one person' with تن used as 'person' here. مباد is an optative/negative form meaning 'may there not be' (classical wish/negative). اندرین = 'in this' (demonstrative + locative), followed by پهن دشت = adjective + noun 'wide plain'. Word order is: subordinating particle → subject → negative-optative → locative phrase → noun phrase.
segments.s615-g59: که again introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'). گوری = 'grave, tomb.' فروشد here functions as a verbal form 'sells / might sell' (classical/poetic third-person form of فروختن); the input senses list does not include the verbal sense, so the grammatical note treats it as a verb in classical narrative. به بازارگان = 'to/for the merchants' (به + بازارگان).
segments.s615-g60: بدیشان is the enclitic-style combination of به + ایشان = 'to them/for them' placed before the verb for emphasis. دهند is third-person plural present/subjunctive of دادن used here in the narrative sense 'they give'/'they would give'. این همه = 'all this' (demonstrative + quantifier) and رایگان = adverb/adjective 'free, for nothing' modifying the verb phrase.
segments.s615-g61: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. بر کوی = 'on/in the quarter/lanes' (بر here used locatively). با نامداران = 'with/among the notable/renowned men' (با = 'with' or 'among', نامداران = 'the renowned'). جز at the end of the segment sets up an exception/limitation to be completed in the next segment ('except' / 'save'). The phrase is elliptical: several locative/agentive pieces precede the verb.
segments.s615-g62: ببردند is third-person plural past narrative 'they took/carried off' (classical narrative form). بسیار functions as a quantifier 'many' here (not merely an intensifier). دیبا و خز are the direct objects listed after the verb: 'brocade (dība) and furs' — standard noun coordination with و.
segments.s615-g63: بپذرفت is third-person singular past 'he accepted' (classical narrative). و فرمود = 'and he said/ordered' (فرمود used as a verb of command). تا here introduces the subordinate clause after a verb of ordering and functions like 'that / so that / in order that' (classical use: فرمود تا + subjunctive). باژ و ساو appear as coordinated items — likely proper names (places) or items; the form and context leave them ambiguous.
segments.s615-g64: نخواهند is a negated form used here to express prohibition/negation in the subordinate clause (classical sense 'they should not / they will not [be allowed to]'). اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. چندشان = چند + ـشان, 'some/many of them' ( چَند is an indefinite/quantifying word with the possessive pronoun). بود is the past form of 'to be' used here in the protasis ('if they were/if there was'). تاو at the end is archaic/ambiguous in this context (its precise function is obscure in the manuscript text and may be a poetical/deictic particle); the clause overall means 'they should not (do X), even if some of them were so/if some of them were his.'
segments.s615-g73: بگفتی here introduces direct speech; in epic Persian this verb form (b‑prefix + past stem) functions as the reporting verb (‘said’) placed before the addressed phrase. که‌ای is an archaic sequence that introduces a vocative: که + ای (O), and دادخواهندگان is a plural agent noun (‘those who seek justice’) in vocative position addressing the audience.
segments.s615-g74: This is the main imperative/jussive clause: پناهید is a second‑person plural jussive/imperative ‘take refuge’ (classical present‑subjunctive form used as command). به یزدان is the prepositional complement ‘in/with God’ (به + proper name), and از بندگان marks the source/contrast (‘from servants/men’) — together: ‘take refuge in God, not in men.’
segments.s615-g75: This is a conditional protasis introduced elliptically. کسی functions as an indefinite subject (‘anyone/someone’), کو here is the classical relative/particle attaching to that indefinite (its exact sense is not one of the provided glosses), and بخفته‌ست is a perfective/state verb ‘lies asleep / has fallen asleep’ (classical compound form). با رنج ما is a prepositional phrase ‘with/ amid our suffering’ modifying the verb.
segments.s615-g76: وگر begins a conditional continuation (‘and/if’), نیستش is the negative existential/possessive construction (نیست + enclitic pronoun ‑ش = ‘he does not have’), بهره is the direct object ‘a share/portion,’ and از گنج ما (‘of/from our treasure’) specifies source/possession: ‘and if he has no share of our treasure.’
segments.s615-g77: به میدان خرامید is a simple past action: به + میدان (‘into/to the field/arena’) followed by خرامید, the past tense of خرامیدن ‘to stride/prance’ — the verb follows its locative phrase as is common in classical diction. تا introduces the purpose clause that follows (‘so that…’), and شهریار is the subject of that subordinate clause (‘the king’).
segments.s615-g78: مگر here functions as a modal/introducer of a hopeful or tentative purpose (classical Persian often uses it in the sense ‘perhaps’/‘that perhaps’ — this nuance is not one of the supplied glosses). بر شما is a beneficiary/target phrase ‘upon/for you,’ نوکند is a third‑person verbal form ‘may/might make new’ (the imperfect/subjunctive sense ‘renew’), and روزگار is the object ‘fortune/time/fate’ — together: ‘(so that) perhaps the king may renew your fortunes.’
segments.s615-g79: دگر begins a contrast (‘else/by contrast’); هرک is an indefinite/relative ‘whoever’ (classical form), and پیرست is the predicate ‘is old’ (an archaic copular/adjectival form). The following و بیکار و سست strings adjectival predicates joined by و (‘and idle and weak’), describing that general class.
segments.s615-g80: همان is an emphatic demonstrative ‘that very one/the same,’ کو again is the classical relative particle attaching to آن/همان (sense not among the provided glosses), and جوانست is the predicate ‘is young.’ The second part (و ناتن درست) joins with و; درست is an adjectival predicate ‘right/proper.’ The lexical item ناتن is uncertain in the supplied senses (treated here as out of-list), so the clause rhythmically contrasts the young (همان) as the proper/fit person.

sentences.s615-s69: From every land they raised a new cry, and they adorned the earth with brocade.
sentences.s615-s70: Thus, for a while the world experienced it — in feasting and in battle, in shame and in combat.
sentences.s615-s25: When they saw that wound on the she-onager, the wise man said, "This is sword and might."
sentences.s615-s26: May the evil eye not fall upon this king; nothing will remain except the moon on the vault of heaven.
sentences.s615-s27: The heads of the great men of the world are beneath him; the sky is under his arrows and sword.
sentences.s615-s28: The army then rushed on after (them); across the plain they sprang from the graves.
sentences.s615-s13: At once the grave came up to his head, and the golden-belted ranks moved forward.
sentences.s615-s14: They stood amazed at that wound of his; one by one they shouted 'bravo.'
sentences.s615-s15: No one saw the feather or the shaft of his arrow; he vanished above that grave.
sentences.s615-s16: The mounted warriors and men bent on vengeance all throughout shouted 'bravo.'
sentences.s615-s53: Thereafter the treasure-house was opened; he who had been in need became wealthy.
sentences.s615-s54: From the hunting-ground he went toward Baghdad; having gained wisdom, he went with a joyful heart.
sentences.s615-s55: The rebels advanced before him — from strangers and those of his own who were ill-disposed.
sentences.s615-s56: He commanded that the army return; the host came to the palace of the beloved king.
sentences.s615-s45: The sinner has an opinion about that matter; in his heart he lacks shame and the fear of God.
sentences.s615-s46: Do not disclose this word/sign to anyone again, for I am among the guardians of secrets and free of need.
sentences.s615-s47: I make the poor man wealthy; I bring the life of the ill‑fated to the faith.
sentences.s615-s48: We give no one even a dirham; there is no money, and his heart is full of sorrow.
sentences.s615-s33: From those towns, whoever was a dervish, or whose bread was earned by his own effort.
sentences.s615-s34: By his giving they became wealthy, and many moreover attained thrones and helmets.
sentences.s615-s35: He came into the city from the hunting-ground; for about a week he was rejoicing with the army.
sentences.s615-s36: A melodious-voiced speaker went forth — a wise man and a dervish-seeker.
sentences.s615-s21: He raised that beast of burden from its place; you said, 'That load became a winged Humā.'
sentences.s615-s22: A certain onager came forward; it was a female. The foal had gone ahead of her; she remained behind.
sentences.s615-s23: A rider struck it in the middle with his sword; the onager became unsteady.
sentences.s615-s24: The chiefs came near him — proud and sword‑wielding young men.
sentences.s615-s41: And if anyone of that company is in debt, he has grown weary of bad creditors.
sentences.s615-s42: And if they are fatherless children as well, they will ask things of that man who has it.
sentences.s615-s43: If there is a mother with a hidden needy child, I will open the treasure twice.
sentences.s615-s44: And if a wealthy, affluent man dies, his small children will be left in this land.
sentences.s615-s5: One champion said, "O king, look upon this renowned host."
sentences.s615-s6: Who is with such arrows and bows? An evil-minded man — think no good of him.
sentences.s615-s7: May this be ample for you, that your head and helmet may be eternal.
sentences.s615-s8: When you take up arrows and (also) sword and mace, from that royal splendour and exalted height...
sentences.s615-s49: If anyone else harbors a hidden need, he still keeps secret the cause of his own distress.
sentences.s615-s50: I will make him carefree of that affair, I will increase his joy and lessen his sorrow.
sentences.s615-s51: If there was hardship even from the officials, it was because he had asked something of a fatherless man.
sentences.s615-s52: I will hang the oppressor alive, for he harmed a free (noble) man.
sentences.s615-s65: From that the King of Iran was deeply troubled; he became angry and, at Rouzbeh, bit his lip.
sentences.s615-s66: He said to him, 'I will raise a cry to Rome and the Khazars; I will give them Badr as a companion.'
sentences.s615-s67: At once demand a kharvar of dinars; from the treasuries of Rey and Isfahan proclaim it with a cry.
sentences.s615-s68: The bedchamber was ruined in this manner; it was not because of the king of Iran's star.
sentences.s615-s61: For two weeks the heart was joyful; day and night he opened the treasury.
sentences.s615-s62: Dirhams were given and came into the city of Istakhr; that Kian set the proud crown upon his head.
sentences.s615-s63: When he opened the door of his chamber, he gave the beauties dirhams from the treasure.
sentences.s615-s64: For anyone who had no crown, (he placed) a golden musk (item) under the inner ivory throne.
sentences.s615-s1: The next day, when Hūr laid down (gave up) the crown, he became sovereign and went toward the onager-hunt.
sentences.s615-s2: The army, having set its bows on the strings (i.e. having strung their bows), marched on; then the king went into their midst.
sentences.s615-s3: He said thus: whoever with his hand rubs the bow, it will open (loosen) by the width of a thumb.
sentences.s615-s4: One must not shoot an arrow except at the cypresses (tall targets) — those for which the arrow comes out from the chest.
sentences.s615-s9: The whole host is ashamed before the king; from their bows and arrows their hands grow weak.
sentences.s615-s10: He answered thus: 'This is divine; compared to it, what is Bahram's might?'
sentences.s615-s11: Shabdiz reared Bahram up; he then made Dolaram swift.
sentences.s615-s12: When the time came, he opened his hand; with his cypresses he bound the grave.
sentences.s615-s17: To him the champion said, “O king, may your eye never see ill days.”
sentences.s615-s18: Your horsemen and all of us are on donkeys; even compared with the Kharurān we are less skilled.
sentences.s615-s19: The king said to him, “This is not my arrow; the victorious one is my helper (protector).”
sentences.s615-s20: There is no one who is the sovereign’s support and helper; there is no one in the world more despicable than he.
sentences.s615-s57: They adorned the golden bedchamber; the worshippers played the stringed instrument and wished.
sentences.s615-s58: The beauties composed verses and harps; they attended to the stranger’s portico.
sentences.s615-s59: From strings and fruit came the sound of the harp and the song; they continually gave the air a greeting.
sentences.s615-s60: Each night they took one bracelet from every chamber, until no heart remained base.
sentences.s615-s29: One man went around the army, so that there would not be a single person in that wide plain.
sentences.s615-s30: that they might sell a grave to the merchants, and give all this to them for free.
sentences.s615-s31: From the quarters of the notable men they took, save for that, many bolts of brocade and furs.
sentences.s615-s32: He accepted and commanded that Baj and Saav should not be taken, even if some of them were (his).
sentences.s615-s37: He said, “O seekers of justice, take refuge in God, not in (mere) men.”
sentences.s615-s38: If anyone lies asleep amid our suffering — and if he has no share of our treasure.
sentences.s615-s39: He strode into the field so that the king might (perhaps) renew your fortunes.
sentences.s615-s40: By contrast, whoever is old is idle and weak; the one who is young is (the) proper (man).
